card.intuition: Average the gradient over a small batch (32–512 samples). Less noisy than SGD (batch=1) so steps are more directional; much faster than full-batch GD. Modern GPUs process mini-batches in parallel — batch size is often a hardware efficiency choice as much as a hyperparameter.
card.analogy: Taking a group poll instead of asking one person (SGD) or everyone (batch GD). A group of 32–256 people gives a reliable-enough signal quickly — individual quirks average out, but you don't need to wait for the entire population.
card.time_complexity: O(B) per step for batch size B; O(n) per epoch
card.space_complexity: O(B · d) to hold one batch in memory
when_to_use: Standard training for most deep learning models
When GPU parallelism can fully utilise the batch size
Balancing between noisy SGD and slow full-batch GD
gotchas: Batch size affects convergence: large batches → sharp minima → worse generalisation (sometimes)
Shuffle data before each epoch to prevent the model from memorising batch order
Effective learning rate scales with batch size: doubling batch size may require doubling lr

exercise.prompt: Implement mini-batch gradient descent for linear regression.
hints: Divide the accumulated gradient by len(batch) to get the average, then scale by lr: lr * grad[j] / len(batch)
